data.title: Kesko Oyj: A Hidden Nordic Retail Giant At A Very Reasonable Valuation
data.description:
Kesko Oyj (KKOYF)(KKOYY) is one of the leading retailers in Northern Europe that has been flying under the radar with international investors. The company transformed itself from a small grocery retailer into what is now a sprawling retail empire dominating multiple verticals. Its main focus continues to be groceries, but it now also operates DIY building and home improvement products stores, along with consumer and fleet car sales. It is a dominant player in Finland, but has expanded to nearby markets such as Sweden, Norway, Denmark, and beyond.

In recent years, the company has strategically invested in digital platforms, logistics infrastructure, and geographic expansion to ensure it maintains a strong competitive moat and that it is well-placed to capture market growth. While it is not a high-growth company, it offers an attractive dividend and has potential for profit margin improvement. It can be an interesting company to follow for investors searching to diversify with international companies operating in stable regions.

Company Overview

As previously mentioned, Kesko is a familiar brand in Finland and some Nordic countries, but it flies under the radar when it comes to international investors. The company can be thought of as a mix of Walmart (WMT), Home Depot (HD), and to a lesser extent CarMax (KMX). Kesko operates three grocery store brands, mostly in Finland, and has expanded to more than 1,100 stores across the country.

Kesko has been quick to adopt online shopping, with online grocery now accounting for a meaningful percentage of revenue. Importantly, Kesko has followed the strategies of the likes of Costco (COST) and Kroger (KR), giving high importance to developing its own private labels, which now account for roughly 20% of total grocery sales. In Finland, Kesko has the second-largest market share with approximately 33%, and over 40% of the online grocery market. Kesko also operates a B2B food service business that caters to restaurants, hotels, and other similar businesses, similar to what Sysco Inc. (SYY) does. The grocery segment is delivering a relatively high operating margin, compared to that of other similar peers.

The second key business segment for Kesko is Building & Technical Trade [BTT], which caters to both professional trades people as well as DIY home improvement customers. It can be thought of as a similar business to Home Depot or Lowe's (LOW). This business operates in eight countries, and currently has a relatively low operating margin, but management has shared their expectations that they can reach their 6-8 % EBIT margin target in a few years. This business is being expanded mostly through bolt-on acquisitions in Northern Europe, and its sales are roughly two-thirds to building professionals and one-third to DIY customers.

Finally, the car trade business segment sells used and new cars. This segment is performing well, but its net sales represent only around 10% of total group net sales.

Bolt-on Acquisitions

Kesko has followed a strategy of M&A growth, in particular for its building and trade [BTT] business segment. Acquisitions have tended to be relatively small and easy to integrate, a kind of roll-over expansion strategy. This has allowed Kesko to quickly gain market share in new markets. A good example is the recent purchase of family-owned Danish retailer Davidsen A/S for €427 million in 2024, one of Denmark's largest building and home improvement retailers. This acquisition was further reinforced with additional smaller acquisitions in 2025 that will be integrated into the Davidsen subsidiary.

First Quarter of FY2025 Results

Kesko's net sales increased modestly to €2.8 billion in the first quarter of FY2025, representing steady year-over-year progress despite this being traditionally Kesko’s slower quarter. Interestingly, the BTT and Car Trade divisions emerged as strong performance standouts. Both showed robust top-line growth, with BTT delivering impressive B2B dynamics, and the Car Trade defying unmistakable market downturn trends. This shows Kesko’s ability to diversify earnings effectively, improving resilience.

Working capital and inventories increased significantly, though largely due to seasonal reasons. The balance sheet remains reassuringly solid, underlined by debt metrics comfortably below management’s targeted ceiling. Margins in the grocery segment have been pressured as the company invests in pricing competitiveness, which is positioning Kesko as consumers’ top choice for affordable quality. This is a case of taking some short-term pain for long-term gain, and there are early signs that the strategy is working with improvements seen in footfall and online basket size.

The Building & Technical Trade is already benefiting from the Danish acquisitions, offering refreshed growth momentum and significant potential for profit margin expansion. As for the Car Trade segment, it continues gaining market share as it benefits from some popular electric vehicle launches from trusted automobile brands.

Financials

Kesko is one of the companies facing a post-pandemic pull-back, as consumers initially splurged after the pandemic lockdowns. This pulled forward demand, which resulted in a subsequent growth decline. Still, it appears things are now stabilizing for the company, and its operating margin remains quite healthy for a retailer.

To put things in perspective, Kesko's operating margin of ~4.6% is higher compared to global peers such as Walmart, Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY), or Tesco PLC (OTCPK:TSCDY). Also, according to management, they expect margins to improve in the medium to long-term.

Kesko might not be a wonderful business, but it is a pretty decent one, with returns on equity of ~15%, and returns on capital employed [ROCE] and returns on invested capital [ROIC] of close to 10%. This might not be the most attractive franchise in the world, but it is a solid business earning very decent returns on its capital employed.

Balance Sheet

Kesko is also managed in a very prudent way, with relatively low leverage and a solid balance sheet. For comparison, global peers like Walmart and Tesco are currently operating with higher debt-to-EBITDA and debt-to-asset multiples.

Global Peers

Kesko Oyj also stands out among global peers, with some of the highest profit margins, exceeding those of companies like Walmart, Costco, and Kroger. Still, as is the case with most retailers, profit margins are low in absolute terms, and below those of U.S. home improvement leader Home Depot.

Shareholder Returns

One key attraction for income investors is that Kesko has a generous dividend policy of distributing between 60% to 100% of its comparable earnings per share. This approach gives the company more flexibility to adjust dividends as earnings change, so investors have to accept that it is normal for the dividend to be lower if earnings experienced a decline compared to the previous year.

At current prices shares are offering a dividend yield that is close to the ten-year average of ~4.7%, and there is potential for the dividend to be increased again if profit margins and earnings start trending higher once again.

Future Outlook

During the first quarter earnings call, management shared their expectation for operating profit to slightly improve in 2025, although the operating environment is expected to remain relatively challenging. Kesko estimates that its comparable operating profit in 2025 will amount to €640 million to €740 million. The profit guidance is based on an estimate of a gradually improving economic cycle in all Kesko operating countries. The company is still operating below its long-term financial targets, which call for a 6% operating margin, and return on capital employed [ROCE] above 14.5%.

The grocery and car trade business segments already exceed this ROCE target, it is the building and technical trade [BTT] segment which is lowering the group's average. Still, there is hope that as this business gets increased operating leverage from the acquisitions, it will be able to improve its financial performance.

Valuation

Trading at a roughly 23.9x GAAP P/E multiple, Kesko shares are not exactly cheap. Still, the company's strong brand, resilient cash generation, and solid track record justify, to a certain degree, a premium valuation. Still, Kesko will have to deliver the promised margin improvement and grow volume to continue justifying this P/E multiple.

Kesko appears expensive when compared to European peers like Tesco and Ahold Delhaize, but compared to U.S. peers like Walmart it looks like a bargain, trading at less than half its EV/EBITDA and Price to Cash Flow from Operations multiples.

Risks

While grocery stores are relatively stable businesses, often considered consumer staples, the company does have several important risks for investors to consider. First, competition in the grocery sector is intense, with operators in Finland like S Group and Lidl competing aggressively on price, which have pushed Kesko to lower prices too. In the Car Trade segment competition is also fierce, both with regional automotive dealerships and international car manufacturers. For its part, the BTT segment is highly cyclical and sensitive to economic conditions. Higher interest rates have dented construction and remodeling activity, impacting this business segment.

Other important risks include potential integration issues, especially international acquisitions, which might have different business cultures. Finally, tariffs and low consumer sentiment pose risks, particularly to the more discretionary or cyclical businesses such as the BTT and Car Trade segments. The risks are mitigated by Kesko's strong brand, particularly in the Nordic countries, a solid balance sheet, and healthy cash generation.

Conclusion

Kesko Oyj is an interesting company that investors interested in international companies should keep on their radar. While the company is currently not cheap, its valuation is very reasonable, and its core grocery business is highly resilient to economic cycles. The company can be particularly interesting to income investors, especially those wishing to diversify their dividends to other currencies, as Kesko has a policy of distributing most of its earnings as dividends. We believe the current valuation is reasonable, but for the time being we are happy to just keep an eye on the company in case an opportunity comes to buy shares at a significant discount to fair value.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: Catching Up In AI Doesn't Make It Undervalued
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has outperformed the market by double-digits, since we recommended selling it, given its overvalued nature. The company has recently announced a $6 billion share buyback and continues to generate substantial profits. Despite that, AMD is an overvalued company that has a minimal ability to generate shareholder returns.

AMD Q1 Financial Results

The company has more than $7 billion in revenue in the quarter, substantial growth as its data center has outperformed.

However, the company still trades at roughly 7x annual revenue, an incredibly lofty valuation with its $190 billion market cap. While peer Nvidia is at 17x annual revenue, we also feel that the valuation of peers is incredibly lofty, as they both face risks. That's a valuation that needs substantial cash flow that can be directed towards shareholder returns. It's also worth noting that the rapid revenue growth seen with the company and datacenters appears to be slowing down.

The company has managed to do this revenue growth while growing its margins. The company's GAAP margins are 50% and the company's non-GAAP margins came in at 54%. The company has been supported by the Ryzen processor, which has become a strong CPU offering that's very competitive with what's offered by Intel.

That has enabled the company to capture increased market share.

The company's slowing growth is visible through 36% YoY growth but a 3% QoQ revenue decline. Even QoQ the company also saw a 1% decline in its GAAP margins. At the same time, the company's expenses, even with the gross margin, remain quite high. That means that the company's net income, despite strong QoQ performance, came in at $700 million.

That puts the company at a P/E of almost 70x. That's tough for a company that declined QoQ.

AMD Segment Performance

The company's segment performance also shows some concern with the company's valuation given that the company would need to hit 4x profits to justify its valuation.

The company's client & gaming did well off of new CPU and GPU releases but the TAM here for the company is dramatically smaller. Embedded has seen consistent weakness, and shrunk in size, though operating income remains strong. Still these are two segments that we don't see a path to justifying the company's valuation with their growth.

That leaves the rapidly growing data center market as the source of potential growth for the company. Here is where the concerns lie. The company saw substantial YoY growth after 2024 was a great year. However, QoQ the company saw a 5% revenue decline and 19% operating income decline. That implies that the company has had to trim margins here to preserve revenue.

Given the slowing down of data center growth, and the company's largest customers building their own CPUs, we don't see this segment ever providing a path to the company justifying its valuation.

AMD Artificial Intelligence

Now, it's worth noting that AMD is investing in a number of AI startups, and introducing the MI350X.

AMD has acquired startups such as Enosemi and Untether designed to help it improve its AI silicon and software offerings around AI. While these offerings will benefit the company, it's worth noting that AMD's 2025 acquisition count is lower than financially wealthier peer Nvidia. As a result, we don't expect this to set it ahead.

Now the MI350 will be a competitive processor and in a rack format will fit 128 processors with 33 kW of power. On a 3nm process versus 4 nm for Blackwell along with high memory (288 gB HBM3E) that makes it a strong processor for AI related tasks. MI350 benchmarks aren't out yet, but the MI325X was on par with Hopper and 1/3 of Blackwell.

Given the strong talked about compute of the MI350 vs MI325X we expect it to be competitive. However, in our view, parity between AMD and Nvidia especially with AMD's investments in these AI startups, is more of a sign of the lofty valuations.

Specifically, these companies need lofty margins to justify their valuation and aggressive revenue growth. However, their primary customers are the mega tech companies (Amazon, Google, etc.) who are all building their own GPUs. Competition between Nvidia and AMD will encourage both to lower margins from current lofty levels, which will hurt profits.

Combined with increased competitors and slowing down margin growth, we see profits never hitting the level required to justify investment.

AMD Shareholder Returns

The company has managed to build up its cash position versus prior years, but it still remains low versus its valuation.

The company had $727 million in FCF, but spent more than that on shareholder returns including share repurchases. This higher than the company's FCF rate is an only 1.5% shareholder return rate. That came with several billion in total debt increases, and it indicates how the company's ability to drive future returns will be tough without substantial growth.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that AMD is working to capture market share from its rivals. It's market share in GPU is 8% versus more than 90% for Nvidia. Capturing substantial market share from peers Nvidia etc. with lower cost offerings could enable the company to drive more substantial shareholder returns and cash flow in excess of the company's current valuation.

Conclusion

AMD has outperformed the market since the last time we recommended selling it. Despite that, we recommend investors not being fooled by the company's valuation. The company's modest share buybacks are only 1.5% annually and the company barely afford it with its current FCF. The company's total debt load has continued to grow as well.

The company had a strong 2024 but QoQ it's clear that the company's growth rate is slowing down. Despite a strong showing with consumer CPUs and GPUs, the datacenter is the only market large enough to justify the company's valuation and growth there is slowing down rapidly. That slowdown in growth will hurt the company's ability to drive additional returns.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Alpha Picks Weekly Market Recap
data.description:
Hello and welcome to our weekly recap. I’m Steven Cress, Head of Quantitative Strategies at Seeking Alpha.

This week, global events took center stage. We saw renewed escalation in the Middle East, a tragic plane crash in India, and an encouraging agreement between the U.S. and China all impacting markets. In this newsletter, we’ll provide insights into these developments and also highlight notable movements within the Alpha Picks portfolio.

I’m also excited to announce the recent launch of our latest Quant product: the PRO Quant Portfolio (PQP). PQP is an actively managed, data-driven model portfolio that harnesses Seeking Alpha’s proprietary Quant system to deliver high-conviction stock ideas with disciplined execution. I encourage all readers to explore this new offering and discover the unique advantages it brings.

Thank you for joining us and I hope you enjoy this week's edition.

Israel-Iran Conflict Roils Global Markets

Regional tensions escalated when Israel launched military action against Iran, causing significant turbulence in global financial markets. Major averages opened lower, with the S&P 500, Nasdaq, and Dow all falling in pre-market. The sell-off also included European and Asian equities, as well as regional markets in the Middle East.

Oil prices saw substantial increases. Brent crude futures (CO1:COM) surged up to 13% in early trading, before moderating later in the trading session. Traditional safe-haven investments gained traction as investors sought stability; both gold prices and the US dollar strengthened.

Source Link

Investors remain concerned the conflict could constrict oil supplies, particularly through the Strait of Hormuz, which is a critical thoroughfare for global energy transport.

Boeing Shares Sink After Fatal Air India Dreamliner Crash

In Ahmedabad, India, the catastrophic crash of an Air India Boeing 787-8 Dreamliner killed nearly all people on board, marking the first fatal Dreamliner crash reported. Boeing's (BA) shares trended lower in the aftermath, along with Indian carriers like IndiGo and SpiceJet. The incident intensifies scrutiny on Boeing's safety record after a litany of challenges over the past several years.

Geopolitical Events

Following two dates of negotiations in London, the U.S. and China reached a preliminary trade agreement on Monday. The deal, which is still awaiting final approval, would see China relax export restrictions on rare earth minerals and magnets. In turn, the U.S. would ease restrictions on advanced technology exports to China and loosen visa restrictions for Chinese STEM students. The markets responded with cautious optimism.

Amid periods of shifting trade policy and geopolitical uncertainty, Quant offers tools that simplify stock picking by analyzing financial data to highlight stocks with the strongest investment fundamentals. Alpha Picks offers a more efficient approach to smarter investing, leveraging Seeking Alpha's powerful quantitative stock-picking system to deliver two data-driven stocks picks each month, helping investors save time and focus on high-quality opportunities.

GICS Sector Performance

Energy (XLE) led sector performance this week, as oil prices jumped following Israel's attack on Iran, with renewed concerns about potential oil supply disruptions.

Major deals in the Healthcare sector ignited sector-wide gains.

Industrials (XLI) finished lower for the week as concerns over lingering trade tensions weighed on the sector.

Crypto & Currencies

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell as much as 3% during early Friday trading, following renewed tensions in the Middle East.

The US dollar (DXY) continued its downtrend for the week, sinking to its lowest level in three years amid market turbulence over shifting US trade policy. However, the currency experienced a slight rally Friday morning following a rush to safe-haven assets.

Alpha Picks

What is Alpha Picks? Alpha Picks provides two monthly data-driven stock picks delivered straight to your inbox. Alpha Picks has collectively gained 168%, compared to the S&P 500’s 60% since its inception in July 2022.

The portfolio continues to significantly outperform, delivering a trailing three-month return of 18.39% compared to 8.03% for the S&P 500. AP stocks in the Financial and Industrial sectors led performance for the week.

United Natural Foods Inc. (UNFI) reported Monday that it had suffered a major cybersecurity breach, forcing the company to take key systems offline and disrupting order fulfillment at major retailers. This was announced one day before UNFI’s strong Q3 FY2025 earnings were announced, with net sales rising 7.5% to $8.06 billion and adjusted EBITDA increasing 20.8%. Despite these stellar results, the breach took a toll on share prices, which declined by double digits for the week. This decline, despite the company’s fundamental strength, creates a potential buying opportunity for investors looking for a bargain.

For investors with a fixed amount of capital who prefer a higher frequency of trading, consider our recently launched PRO Quant Portfolio (PQP). PQP is an actively managed, data-driven model portfolio that leverages Seeking Alpha’s proprietary Quant system to deliver high-conviction stock ideas through a disciplined, systematic rebalancing process. With weekly alerts, transparent buy/sell recommendations, and a focus on outperforming the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP), PQP empowers active investors seeking long-term capital appreciation decisions without the need for constant capital infusions or manual intervention.

Economic Rundown

  • FOMC Outlook: Markets are pricing in a 97% chance the Federal Reserve will keep interest rates unchanged at 4.25%-4.50% during next week’s FOMC meeting. Rate cut expectations have now shifted to September, where there is a 56% probability rates will be cut 25bps.

  • Inflation: The May CPI rose 0.1% M/M, coming in below consensus forecasts. Year-over-year, the index rose 2.4% in May, up from April’s 2.3%, but still below the 2.5% expected.

  • Labor Market: Initial jobless claims held steady at 248K for the week ended June 7, while continuing claims rose to 1.956M, both above consensus expectations. The four-week moving average reached its highest level since August 2023, suggesting some softening in the U.S. labor market.

Seeking Alpha Earnings Calendar

data.title: FTAI Aviation: A Roller Coaster Ride In 2025
data.description:
Shares of aircraft and aircraft engine management concern FTAI Aviation Ltd. (NASDAQ:FTAI) have been on a wild ride in 2025. A short report from research concern Muddy Waters to start 2025 is largely responsible, claiming its shift from an emphasis on aviation leasing to its higher-margin MRO work is simply accounting shenanigans. With some of its aircraft being sold to a joint-venture – in which it participates – with exclusivity on all MRO work accruing to FTAI, the recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview

FTAI Aviation Ltd. is a Cayman Islands domiciled, New York City headquartered owner, lessor, seller, manufacturer, and repairer of aviation equipment and aftermarket engine components. At YE24, its portfolio held 109 aircraft and 312 engines. With roots dating back to 1946, FTAI was founded in 2014 as Fortress Transportation and Infrastructure Investors and went public in 2015, raising net proceeds of $351.1 million at $17 per share. A series of transactions were effectuated in 2022 to change its structure from an LLC to a corporation, at which point the current moniker was adopted. Shares of FTAI trade just under $124.00 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization of $12.7 billion.

Approach

The company’s business consists of two operating segments: Aviation Leasing and Aerospace Products. Aviation Leasing is fairly straightforward: acquire aircraft and aircraft engines and subsequently market them for lease or sell, further supported by its maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) operations (a.k.a. Aerospace Products). Leveraging its refurbishment and manufacturing capacity, FTAI can purchase an engine, refurbish it to sell or strip several of them for their parts, and reconfigure a new one from components that have not timed out. The engines that are the primary focus of FTAI are older with many in the unserviceable category. They have three modules (low-pressure turbine, core, and fan) comprised of 18 ‘life-limited’ parts that must be replaced/refurbished after an FAA-mandated set of cycles (takeoffs and landings). FTAI is attempting to leverage its MRO operation where it can mix-and-match life-limited parts with similar remaining life spans to create an engine for sale (or lease) to airlines at a significant profit. All of this functionality was located at an ex Pan-Am engine shop in South Florida until the company purchased an underutilized 526,000-square-foot aircraft engine maintenance facility in Montreal, Canada from Lockheed Martin (LMT) for $170 million in September 2024. A 50% ownership interest in an ex-Alitalia MRO facility located in Rome, Italy is expected to close in Q2 '25, eventually bringing the company’s total maintenance/refurbishment capacity to 600 engines (1,800 modules) annually.

Returning to Aviation Leasing, its typical clients are small and medium airlines, defined as less than 100 engines and 100 to 500 engines, respectively. It also houses a maintenance operation that generates significant revenue. The segment was responsible for FY24 net income from continuing operations of $210.2 million and Adj. EBITDA of $500 million on revenue of $628.4 million.

Aerospace Products consists of development and manufacture, repair/refurbishment, and sale of aircraft engines and aftermarket components for CFM56-7B, CFM56-5B, and V2500 commercial engines, which encompass ~60% of the commercial narrowbody jets in service globally. This segment currently has a 5% share of the $22 billion MRO business associated with those three engine types. However, management is looking to eventually expand this business significantly (as evidenced by its two recent MRO acquisitions) to 25%. If the facilities in Miami, Montreal, and Rome are at full capacity – a goal that will take several years – FTAI could command a 20% market share. Aerospace Products contributed FY24 net income from continuing operations of $346.3 million and Adj. EBITDA of $381 million on revenue of $1.08 billion.

Putting it all together with corporate overhead, FTAI earned $2.13 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $862.1 million on revenue of $1.73 billion in FY24. The bottom line is challenging to tease out, given that 2024 was the year the company became a pure play aviation concern, but for comparison purposes, FY23 Adj. EBITDA from continuing operations was $597.3 million on revenue of $1.17 billion, as it made significant investments into its aviation business in 2024.

Share Price Performance

FTAI was one of the best performing stocks coming out of the pandemic-induced selloff, surging 5,666% from a low of $3.15 a share to an all-time high of $181.64 on January 10, 2025. Then on January 15, 2025, Muddy Waters issued a short report on the company, accusing it of misleading financial reporting, stating that it was representing one-time engine sales (i.e., straight trading without any overhaul or module swapping) as MRO revenue (i.e., three individual module sales) in its Aerospace Products segment in an attempt to garner higher multiples than that of a traditional leasing business.

Distilling down on the short report, an MRO generally trades at a multiple to Adj. EBITDA, which is generally a higher valuation than that of a leasing business, which is typically evaluated through a price-to-book value lens. FTAI’s mix-and-match module ‘solution’ is essentially designed to create more attractive engines for the end user; specifically, ones in which all the life-limited parts time out simultaneously. In addition to suggesting that 80% of its ‘module’ swaps were whole engine sales – backed by testimony of an alleged former employee – the report suggested that the aforementioned purchase of the Lockheed MRO shop was cosmetic, as that specific operation has always struggled for business. That said, these accusations seem a bit fantastical. For example, if one life-limited part needs to be replaced in one of three modules to bring an engine back to serviceability, and it is then resold – counting as a sale of three modules – that still means the engine in question had to enter the MRO shop for the upgrade. Because the other two modules didn’t require work, the accuracy of the company’s claim that it serviced and sold three modules is the adjudication of the would-be investor.

FTAI was also accused of manipulating the depreciation schedule to generate higher EBITDA margins for its more highly valued – on any applicable multiple basis – Aerospace Products segment at the expense of its lower-valued Aviation Leasing segment. Without getting too into the weeds, this alleged manipulation has the effect of lowering the Aerospace Products segment’s purchase price for what the company deems unserviceable engines – a significant source of its engine modules. Either way, with the market suddenly buying into the narrative of FTAI being a leasing business disguised as an MRO, shares of FTAI plunged 24% in the subsequent trading session to $116.08, eventually bottoming at $75.06 on January 21st, off 59% from its all-time high in six trading sessions.

Further conflating matters for the market was the company’s Strategic Capital Initiative [SCI]. Announced at YE24, through partnerships, the enterprise allows FTAI to participate in the acquisition of on-lease narrowbody aircraft at scale. The first partnership, to which the company is contributing ~$100 million of capital, is purchasing 46 on-lease aircraft from FTAI for $549 million, with FTAI exclusively providing replacement aircraft engines and modules for the term of the collaboration. This arrangement is designed to transform FTAI into an asset-light business, highlighting the MRO segment of its operations.

The stock has been on a wild ride ever since. Management denied the Muddy Waters’ allegations and FTAI’s stock worked its way back to $158.83 a share by February 20th, the date it announced the completion of a board-directed independent audit with no 10-K filing delay. That said, a lackluster Q4 '24 financial reports featuring a revenue miss of $18.2 million on February 26th caused shares of FTAI to reverse course, trading to as low as $81.45 on April 7th.

Q1 '25 Financials & Outlook

They had bounced back to a closing price of $107.11 on April 30, 2025, when the company released its Q1 '25 financials, posting earnings of $1.06 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $268.6 million on revenue of $502.1 million, versus $0.43 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $164.1 million on revenue of $326.7 million. The top line missed Street consensus by $65.6 million, although that was likely due to the timing of asset sales to the SCI partnership.

Management’s FY25 outlook remained unchanged at free cash flow generation of $650 million on Adj. EBITDA of $1.10 billion to $1.15 billion from its two operating segments (i.e., excluding corporate overhead). Although the conference call was rather upbeat, the market tanked shares of FTAI 19% lower to $86.90 in the subsequent trading session.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

Another minor concern is the company’s net leverage, which with debt of $3.64 billion and cash of $112 million, stood at 3.7 on March 31, 2025. However, it is projected to fall to the lower end of a 3.0 to 3.5 range by YE25. FTAI pays a quarterly dividend of $0.30 a share for a current yield of 1.3%, but growth capital and debt retirement are its chief priorities for its FY25 operating cash flow.

Despite the significant turbulence in 2025, sell-side analysts are currently unanimously bullish on the company’s prospects. Since May, seven analyst firms, including BTIG, Morgan Stanley, and RBC Capital have reissued Buy ratings on the stock. Price targets proffered range from $123 to $300 a share, with the majority in the $150 to $190 range.

On average, they expect FTAI to earn $4.69 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $2.31 billion in FY25, followed by $6.77 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $2.83 billion in FY26.

Both CEO Joe Adams and CFO David Moreno appear clear on the company’s path forward, having purchased 9,580 shares between them with a $94 handle on May 2, 2025.

Verdict

Muddy Waters objections notwithstanding, the shifting of the company’s aircraft assets off of its balance sheet through joint ventures with MRO exclusivity with an endgame of lowering its debt and concentrating on commanding a larger share of the higher-margin MRO business is a sound one.

FTAI now trades at just under 27 times forward earnings. This is a slight premium to that of the overall S&P 500. It should be noted, FTAI has much faster sales and earnings growth projected than the overall index. That said, the stock is up just over 40% since the start of May

In this topsy-turvy market, I am not willing to chase that rally. If the next market decline takes FTAI back down to the low to mid $100s where the stock's valuation would be roughly in line with the S&P 500 index, I will initiate a small position in FTAI via covered call orders at that point in time.

data.title: SCHO: The Case For Rotating Out Of Short-Duration Treasuries
data.description:
The Schwab Short-Term U.S. Treasury ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHO) is a fixed income strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the short-end of the Treasury bond yield curve.

The performance of this portfolio strategy will be sensitive to the yield curve, which has gradually become less appealing at the 2-year & 3-year duration in the last year.

Taking into consideration the yield curve, SCHO may be positioned to underperform the ultrashort-end of the curve as well as long-duration Treasuries in terms of income generation. In general, the price of a bond is valued inverse to its respective yield, making the 2-3-year Treasuries more expensive to own while offering investors less earning potential. As a result of this phenomenon, an investor may realize higher returns by investing with a barbell strategy, or investing in ultrashort- and long-duration Treasuries, rather than in short-term Treasuries.

Nonetheless, SCHO has provided investors with significantly more stability in terms of performance when compared to its intermediate (SCHR) and long-duration (SCHQ) bond funds. This is likely the result of the transmutation of the yield curve, which has significantly driven up the yield for long-duration bonds relative to the 2-3 year Treasuries. Essentially, this means that the short-term Treasuries held appreciated in value inverse to the rate decline.

Because investments are meant to be valued in a forward-looking manner, rotating into a long-duration strategy may be more optimal when considering the yield curve.

Overall, the Federal Reserve isn’t expected to cut the Federal Funds Rate in the June 2025 meeting and rather, potentially make the first cut in the September and/or December quarters. 80% of economists are expecting the Fed to cut the key rate to 3.75-4%, down from the current range of 4.25-4.50%.

This could result in a short-term return on the ultrashort-to-short end of the curve, making long-dated Treasuries a more appealing investment in terms of yield. A rate cut could impact SCHO by increasing the value of short-term Treasuries while lowering the income for new issuances. If a rate cut does occur, I believe it would be prudent to rotate into long-duration Treasuries vs. short-duration, providing investors with greater income exposure.

SCHO was designed to provide investors with exposure to the Treasury bond market with a duration of 1-3 years. Looking at the individual assets, roughly 57% of the portfolio has a duration of 1-2 years with the remainder in 2-3 years.

Accordingly, SCHO has a weighted average maturity of 2 years.

SCHO has a low-cost structure, charging investors a mere 3bps. This is in part with peer short-duration bond funds with similar strategies, including SPDR Short Term Treasury ETF (SPTS), Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Index (VCSH), and Invesco Short Term Treasury ETF (TBLL).

SCHO has a weighted average coupon of 2.97% and has an average yield to maturity of 3.93%. The portfolio has a turnover rate of 69.38%, potentially adding certain duration risk to the strategy. This can be seen in the distribution rate history over the last two years, declining to $0.0874/share in the most recent monthly distribution at the beginning of June 2025. This annualizes to $1.0488/share, yielding approximately 4.32% on a forward basis.

This compares to Schwab’s long-duration Treasury strategy, SCHQ, which has been exhibiting increases to its monthly distributions, annualized at $1.7316/share, yielding approximately 5.52% on a forward basis.

Of the two options, it might be worth considering the beginning of a rotation into long-duration treasuries. Given that a rate cut may occur later in the year or next year, there may remain some value to extract from SCHO; however, for income purposes, SCHQ would potentially hold the higher ground.

Risks Related to SCHO

SCHO only invests in short-duration US Treasuries, exposing investors to the short-end of the yield curve. Though SCHO has exhibited stability in past performance, short-duration US Treasuries are exposed to changes in the Federal Funds Rate, potentially resulting in low-to-no growth during periods of rate stability. In the instance of the Fed cutting the key rate, SCHO may exhibit increased value as the existing bond book will have a more appealing yield when compared to new issuances. In turn, if rates were to rise in the case of higher inflationary pressures following the implementation of the US tariff policies, the value of short-duration Treasuries may decline.

Investors must also take into consideration duration risk. Duration risk is associated with bonds’ sensitivity to changes in interest rates. If the key rate is set to be reduced, the value of short-term bonds may appreciate. In turn, portfolio turnover must be considered as new issuances may provide investors with a lower yield, or a lower income component, making these assets less appealing when compared to higher-yielding investments.

Given the current market conditions, I recommend SCHO with a hold rating. I believe that it may be a good time for investors to rotate into longer duration Treasuries given the more appealing yield.

data.title: ADMA Biologics: Slowing Growth Warrants A Shift To Neutral (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Reassessment Of ADMA Biologics' Recent Results

This article serves as an update to my previous coverage of ADMA Biologics (NASDAQ:ADMA). My initial "Strong Buy" rating was based on the company's strong position in the biologics market, with the unique profile of its flagship drug, Asceniv, and a clear path to significant revenue growth and profitability. Since writing my article, ADMA stock price rose 23% while the S&P 500 remained flat.

ADMA has so far delivered impressive year-over-year growth. However, a trend has emerged in recent quarters that cannot be ignored. One can notice a flattening of sequential revenue. In my opinion, for a growth stock, momentum is key, and this recent stagnation raises questions about the company's near-term trajectory. While the long-term potential remains, I believe a more cautious, neutral stance is now better suited until the company can demonstrate a return to strong quarter-over-quarter growth. The long-term story might still be intact, but the current risk/reward profile is less compelling for new investment.

The fundamental strengths of ADMA that I previously described still exist. Asceniv remains, in my opinion, the best-in-class treatment for patients with primary immunodeficiency due to its unique high-titer antibody profile. For more information, check my previous article. The company is vertically integrated, with a growing network of plasma collection centers ensuring a supply of the critical raw material for its products. The total addressable market for Asceniv is substantial; the company believes they can reach 1 billion in annual revenues before 2030.

Despite those strong tailwinds, the numbers from recent financial reports paint a picture of slowing momentum. Let's look at the revenue over the last several quarters:

Q2 2024: $107.20 million

Q3 2024: $119.84 million

Q4 2024: $117.50 million

Q1 2025: $114.80 million

While year-over-year growth for Q1 2025 was a respectable 40%, the quarter-over-quarter trend is undeniable. After strong growth, revenue has essentially plateaued. In its most recent quarter (Q1 2025), ADMA also missed consensus revenue estimates, which can be a red flag for a company priced for high growth. This slowdown suggests that there could be near-term challenges, whether in sales execution, market penetration, or lower-than-expected demand. I believe one or even two quarters of weak sequential sales is acceptable, but here we had 3 "weak" quarters. I believe there is something more here than just an "off-quarter."

Positive Catalysts On The Horizon

It wouldn't do ADMA justice not to acknowledge the positive developments that could reignite growth. In April 2025, ADMA announced FDA approval for a new yield-enhancement process. This is a significant achievement since it will allow the company to produce approximately 20% more immunoglobulin from the same volume of plasma. This, I believe, will significantly expand margins and, eventually, revenue-if there's enough demand for Asceniv. However, management has indicated that the financial impact of this approval will not be realized until late 2025 and into 2026. Thus, I believe we are still some quarters away from this being reflected in earnings reports. Currently, I believe growth is the most important factor to justify the current stock price.

Second, the company raised its full-year 2025 revenue guidance to "more than $500 million" and its 2026 guidance to "more than $625 million."

While this is a sign of confidence from management, I prefer to see actual growth in earnings reports (QoQ) rather than just long-term guidance. Additionally, I'd like to point out that in the latest earnings report, ADMA missed sales guidance.

Valuation

For fiscal years 2025 and 2026, the company is guiding for adjusted net income to exceed $175 million and $245 million, while revenues are expected to exceed $500 million and $625 million. Additionally, ADMA plans to reach $1 billion in annual revenues before 2030. Adjusted net income was quite close to net income (Picture 1), so in my calculation, I will estimate that their future net income will be the same as their estimated adjusted net income.

I did calculations for bullish and neutral scenarios. In the neutral scenario, I estimated growth will slow due to revenues being flat in the last 3 quarters. I estimated the net margin would improve due to the yield-enhancement process. I believe a 20% improved yield will improve net income by 20%.

In the bull case, I followed the company's sales guidance while estimating an improved net income margin due to the yield-enhancement process and a little bit extra due to cost efficiencies that should materialize with higher sales.

I'd also like to point out that according to DrugPatentWatch, ADMA has patent protection until 2035; thus, I used lower ending price multiple ratios [PE] than I would otherwise.

In my calculation, I could be wrong with the ending multiple. Markets might assign ADMA a higher price multiple, but with patents expiring around 2035, I don't believe a much higher multiple is justified. Still, it will depend on how fast the competition will be able to market generics.

According to my calculation, in the bull case, there is still some upside, but I believe too little, and capital is better invested in some other company. Thus, I sold my position. I want to be clear here; I would not want to short this stock. It's just better to avoid or take profits.

Risks

The primary risk now, in my view, is the failure to re-accelerate revenue growth. If the current plateau continues for another one or two quarters, it could signal a more profound issue with market penetration or competitive dynamics. While the yield enhancement is a positive, it is not a demand-side catalyst. The company still needs to sell the additional products it will produce. The second long-term risk is patent expiration.

Plan

I believe now we are in a good spot to take profits. I might rebuy it if I see QoQ revenue growth reacceleration or if the stock does a pullback. In case I get back in, I will post or write an article.

data.title: United Natural Foods: Temporary Setback, Long-Term Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

United Natural Foods, Inc.’s (NYSE:UNFI) stock price has seen a sharp correction post recent earnings. While the company’s underlying results were strong, investors were spooked by the uncertainty related to business disruption from a recent IT security incident. While I understand the near-term headwind from this incident, I believe investors are missing the forest for the trees when they are overlooking the strong revenue and margin performance from last quarter that indicate good progress towards meeting the company’s long-term turnaround targets. The stock’s valuation is attractive compared to its peer, Performance Food Group Company (PFGC), on an EV/EBITDA basis and, given its good growth prospects, I rate it a buy.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

UNFI recently reported its Q3 2025 results, where it posted 7.5% Y/Y growth. The company has been seeing Y/Y growth acceleration since Q3 2024 as it increased focus on new business addition and increasing penetration among its existing customers, who helped it offset tough macro environment. The current quarter results were a continuation of this trend.

Segmentwise, Wholesale was up 7.5% Y/Y with 12% Y/Y growth in the natural products category while 2.1% Y/Y growth in the conventional category. Wholesale volumes were up 4% Y/Y. The retail segment also saw a recovery in sales after the down last few quarters, with sales increasing 0.4% Y/Y or 1.5% Y/Y on a same-store basis.

Looking forward, I am optimistic about UNFI's growth prospects. UNFI didn’t raise its guidance on the recent earnings call due to a recent IT security incident. However, if we exclude its impact, the underlying business is accelerating.

The company reported 4% Y/Y volume growth in Q3, which is a sequential improvement over the prior quarters. Almost half of this growth came from new business wins, indicating the company gained profitable market share. The company is becoming more disciplined about the business it wants to target. Management is targeting a $90 bn slice of the natural, organic, specialty, and ethnic market, where the retailers are trying to differentiate themselves and the company can serve them profitably.

Last quarter, the company entered into a mutual agreement with Key Food to end their Northeastern distribution partnership. This was an unprofitable relationship for UNFI, and by ending it, the company can focus on diverting resources to more profitable clients.

Overall, management noted that they are tracking ahead of their plans in meeting the three-year targets they set last year, which makes me confident about the company’s medium-term growth trajectory.

One thing that is worrying some investors in the near term is the recent IT system security incident when the company discovered unauthorized activity on certain IT systems on June 5th and took their systems offline. While the company has implemented workarounds to continue serving customers and is focused on safely bringing the systems online, the disruption is still continuing and is expected to impact sales in the current quarter. However, I am not too worried about it. I believe it is a temporary setback, and the company should be able to rectify it over the next couple of weeks.

Investors focusing too much on this disruption might be missing the forest for the trees. The bigger picture remains strong underlying business momentum and good progress towards management's three-year targets. Hence, I remain optimistic about the company’s growth prospects.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

In Q3 2025, the company saw an adjusted EBITDA margin increase of 22 bps Y/Y to 1.95% as it benefited from operating leverage on higher sales, innovation, and efficiency initiatives, including value-adding supplier go-to-market programs and deploying lean principles. This was partially offset by unfavorable mix due to lower margin wholesale rates.

The company is doing a good job in terms of implementing Lean Daily Management and has rolled it out in 20 of their 52 distribution centres. This has resulted in injury rates being down, out-of-stocks improving by 75%, less shrinkage and better throughput. This has helped the company post 21% Y/Y growth in adjusted EBITDA, which was much higher than revenue growth. I expect the company’s EBITDA to continue outpacing revenue growth in the medium term as it implements Lean Daily Management in other distribution centres as well.

The company is also doing a good job in terms of improving margin mix, as is evident from the recent termination of the unprofitable Key Food contract. So, an improved mix should also help the margins in the medium term.

From the near-term perspective, there are some margin headwinds in Q1 due to an IT security incident and one-time contract termination fees of $53 mn for the Key Food contract. However, as discussed in the revenue section, the IT system should be back online in the next couple of weeks, and the hit from it should be temporary. For the Key Food contract, management expects the payback period for termination fees should be less than a year, and I believe it should free up the company resources to chase more profitable growth opportunities.

So, if one can look beyond the current quarter, the margin growth outlook is positive.

Valuation & Ratings

UNFI Stock is trading at 26.35x FY25 (ending July) consensus EPS estimates of $0.82 and 17.44x FY26 consensus EPS estimates of $1.24.

While this is a premium compared to its peer PFGC, which is trading at 19.99x FY25 (ending June) consensus EPS estimates and 16.76x FY26 consensus EPS estimates, one needs to understand that UNFI’s EPS growth over the next few years is going to be much higher than PFGC as its executes its turnaround plans and delever its balance sheet, increasing operating earnings and reducing interest costs.

On an EV/EBITDA basis, the stock’s valuation is lower than Performance Foods. While PFGC is trading at TTM EV/EBITDA of 14.26x and forward EV/EBITDA of 12.24x, UNFI is trading at 8.97x and 8.70x TTM and forward EV/EBITDA, respectively.

The company’s quant valuation grade is also A+, indicating an attractive valuation. As the company continues to progress on its turnaround initiatives, I believe the stock can see a good upside. Hence, I rate the stock a buy.

Risks

The company is executing well on its turnaround and deleveraging initiative, but if there are any issues with its execution in future and the turnaround/deleveraging timeline is extended, it may negatively impact the stock price.

The company has significant exposure (~23% of sales) to Amazon's (AMZN) Whole Foods Market. Any disruption in its relationship with the grocer may negatively impact its results.

data.title: AT&T: Building The Right Way
data.description:
AT&T (T) has received a whole lot of criticism for the original shopping spree that not only got it into cellular but also brought a lot of problems it spent the last few years solving. The fact is that the company made it from a landline company to what it is today. That alone is something many companies in declining industries do not accomplish. Just look at the steel industry, for example. Not only is the company I worked for, Bethlehem Steel, not around, but virtually the whole industry was replaced by brand-new companies like Nucor (NUE). Even though I was told I would never need another job, the future was very different. AT&T deserves a lot of credit for getting to the present in a viable form. It could have done better. But it did well just to survive to become a viable investment option again.

Acquisition Announcement

Now, the latest acquisition announcement brought a whole lot of cries of "not again". But the acquisition of the business from Lumen (LUMN) is very different from past mistakes. Rather than "proving" that a company never learns from the past, this acquisition very much shows that the company did learn from its past to build upon the future.

Michael Porter covers this in his book "Competitive Strategies" for those who want more details. David Dreman covers this from an investing viewpoint in his "Contrarian Investment" book series.

Unlike the past acquisitions that caused a lot of issues and investor pain, this acquisition expands the current business that the company knows very well. Furthermore, the acquisition is relatively small at less than $6 billion in cash. The acquisition is also focused on the fiber business of AT&T. This is a business that the company wants to grow.

That means that the company is not expanding into a new business. Rather, the company is expanding a business it knows well into new areas. Management has also announced that they will joint venture in this area so that investors will still see guidance maintained.

That rather smart joint venture announcement keeps the dividend on solid ground. Otherwise, there is a chance that the company would have to cut the dividend to integrate these operations while "bringing them up to acceptable standards". Given the outcry some years back when the dividend was cut, the joint venture was the obvious option forward. The deal remains accretive while investors are not upset.

Furthermore, relatively small acquisitions in areas well known by management have a far better history of success. Remember that the shopping spree of the past got this company into new areas with each acquisition. Furthermore, some of those acquisitions were not well run to begin with. Turning around such a situation is "easier said than done" as Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) just demonstrated. This acquisition eliminated the rather fraught pathway (thank goodness).

Guidance

Fiscal year 2025 guidance was reinforced. Remember that with all the divestments, downsizing, and refocusing, the company has a relatively new cash pattern where the first quarter was the weakest, and the fourth quarter will likely be the most significant. Some competitors have had this pattern for a while. But this newly refocused company had other businesses that clouded the cash flow picture that exists now.

What management is doing is reinforcing that the new cash pattern will hold again this year because the company is "not going anywhere". Instead, it intends to do what it does best while adding small deals as they become available.

But since the company is already a dominant force in the fiber industry, the rollup type deals like the one that just happened will likely be few and far between. But they could potentially add a percent or maybe two percent to the long-term growth rate.

Last Article

The last article noted that the company posted free cash flow in the first quarter for the second time since all targeted the divestments completed. This is the company's weakest cash flow quarter. So positive free cash flow in the first quarter bodes well for the rest of the fiscal year.

What should be becoming apparent to investors is that the debt ratio and free cash flow make this company far healthier than it was. To this day, it surprises me that the debt rating agencies did not at least issue a warning about the situation. Then again, management clearly solved the problem on its own.

This brings to the discussion that one of the characteristics of investment grade debt rated companies is that they generally resolve their issues before the public realizes that things have gotten past serious. The only real surprise was the lack of focus on cash that Warner Bros Discovery found after they got control of the acquired assets. It may be why there is a relatively new CFO here at AT&T.

Summary

The current management is proving to be quite a bit more agile and flexible than some managements of the past. The announcement of a small acquisition is a case in point for those pointing to much tighter management control. Clearly, in my opinion, this management is not about to repeat the mistakes of the past.

The case remains for a strong buy recommendation as a growth and income idea. Investors can continue to expect decent single digit business growth combined with a dividend yield to see a total return likely in the low teens over time from the current price.

There was a recovery potential that has since been essentially realized. That is replaced with the idea that this stock is likely to be recession resistant. In fact, earnings are likely to continue to grow through any recession. This is likely to be one of the last companies affected by a business downturn.

Risks

There is a complacency risk with a big company like this that competes in an oligarchy where competition is not what it is in a highly fractured industry. As a result, large companies like this one are often forever on the "comeback trail" because they get complacent every time the comeback is finished. It seems like the next CEO forever takes "easy street" which puts the CEO after that on cleanup duty.

This latest acquisition has a very low chance of failure. The risk is that management goes on another shopping spree, as it did in the past that got the company into a bunch of businesses it had no business even looking at in the first place. Sometimes "shopping sprees" go to management's heads, as if they can do "nothing wrong" until the next management details the mess and cleanup strategy.

data.title: Mako Mining: Operating One Of The World's Highest Grade Open-Pit Mines
data.description:
Summary

At the request of a long-time follower, I continue my research within the precious metals mining industry with Mako Mining (TSXV:MKO:CA) (OTCQX:MAKOF), a small gold producer operating the high-grade San Albino open-pit mine in Nicaragua. Given its low operating costs, the free cash flows coming from San Albino can then be reinvested in growth opportunities, either through the drill bit or inorganically. I view Mako's self-funding business model as interesting, and I must admit that I am quite impressed by the company's tight control over executive compensation and administrative costs. As illustrated by the acquisition of the Moss Mine for a net cash acquisition cost of ~$2 million, management appears to manage capital quite efficiently.

However, before discussing Mako in more detail, I would like to say a few words on the geopolitical developments of the last few days, given their important implication for precious metals prices. It is my personal opinion that today's events in the Middle East could mark the beginning of something much larger and consequential than what most people expect.

Middle East Geopolitics

I am writing these lines as oil prices are spiking by almost 8% after Israel conducted military strikes on Iran. As discussed in a previous article about the containership leasing company Danaos Corporation (DAC) in November 2024, a hot war between Israel and Iran could have serious implications for the Strait of Hormuz, the jugular vein of the world economy. According to various news outlets, Iran's armed forces are already considering the closure of Hormuz. Without a surprise, the probability of such an event has increased sharply on the Polymarket betting platform. What was considered a tail risk a few months ago is now becoming front and center.

The deteriorating conditions in the Middle East are slowly putting the Strait of Hormuz into focus. Even though this route is absolutely vital for oil trade, it is less so for containership. In my view, the real deal when it comes to geopolitical risk is the Taiwan Strait, and if Hormuz is the world's jugular vein for oil, the Taiwan Strait and the South China Sea are the jugulars for containerships. Bujon Capital - November 2024

Over the years, Iran has always been clear that in the event of a direct conflict with Israel, it would shut down maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz. As a reminder, approximately 20% of the world's oil consumption flows through this narrow passage, and we can all imagine the potential impacts of its closure on oil prices. The entire globe would be subject to another wave of inflation, potentially larger than the one we have all experienced following the lockdowns of March 2020.

In turn, increasing inflation expectations may lead to additional selling pressure on the Treasury market. After all, the United States is already running massive fiscal deficits, and another war in the Middle East would only make the problem worse. Interest expenses are rapidly compounding, and the feedback loop of debt issuances is becoming increasingly obvious. The United States and many other Western countries are finding themselves in a debt trap, so why would investors seek safety in their sovereign bonds?

More and more people are starting to ask those hard questions, and once critical mass is reached, their herding instinct could fuel a historic bull market in precious metals. After all, precious metals held in physical form are one of the few asset classes without counterparty risks. After reading the book 'The Great Taking' by David Rogers Webb, I cannot say the same about paper gold traded on financial markets, unfortunately. Clearing houses may become the focal point during the next financial crisis, and this is precisely when the notion of counterparty risks could come back with a vengeance in the public's consciousness.

For reasons highlighted in the research paper 'Resilience redux in the US Treasury market' published in August 2023, the Federal Reserve would be forced to intervene in case of a severe dislocation in the Treasury market. Ultimately, ensuring market stability in their respective sovereign bond markets is one of the primary functions of central banks. After another round of money printing, what would happen to precious metal prices? Given the record amount of debt outstanding, the required bailout would have to be larger than the previous one. This is how the current monetary system operates: akin to a worldwide Ponzi scheme. By focusing my research efforts on the precious metals mining industry, my hope is to be at the forefront of what could be a historic bull market. It is from this geopolitical and macroeconomic standpoint that I approach Mako Mining.

High-Grade Mining In Nicaragua

Mako's flagship asset is the high-grade San Albino mine in Nicaragua. With a measured and indicated resource grade averaging 11.61 g/t, San Albino is one of the world's highest grade open-pit mines. In the first quarter of 2025, Mako milled ore with an average grade of 7.1 g/t, but that includes dilution from lower-grade zones. It is quite interesting that I am referring to an open-pit gold grade of almost 3 g/t as 'lower grade'. Of course, this is only true when compared to ore grading in excess of 10 g/t from diluted veins.

When I think about high-grade open-pit mines, the first name that comes to my mind is B2Gold's (BTG) Goose Project in Nunavut. As discussed in a previous article about the company in November 2024, Goose is estimated to host ~6.3 million ounces of gold at an average grade of 5.88 g/t, which compares to San Albino's ~250,000 ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 11.61 g/t. In addition, production volumes at Goose are expected to surpass 300,000 ounces per year in the coming year, while San Albino produced just short of 40,000 ounces in 2024. Finally, Goose is located in one of the harshest and most remote places on Earth, while San Albino benefits from a warm climate and proximity to infrastructure. They are two completely different assets, but their comparison showcases San Albino's small-scale and impressive resource grade.

With a resource estimate of approximately 250,000 ounces of gold and a production volume of ~40,000 ounces in 2024, San Albino has a reserve-life index just above 6.0 years. In May 2025, Mako announced some interesting drill holes following exploration work on adjacent properties, including 39.15 g/t Au and 27.8 g/t Ag over 8.0 m and 18.14 g/t Au and 9.5 g/t Ag over 7.0 m.

How long will San Albino remain in production? Nobody can truly know, but I think Mako is likely to find additional milling material before the known resources are exhausted. If everything goes well, could Mako continue to report impressive margins from San Albino for another 5+ years? Yes, probably. After all, considering that commercial production at San Albino started in July 2021, it is still a relatively young asset.

As illustrated below, San Albino was brought to production following a relatively low amount of drilling of approximately 100,000 meters. With healthy cash flows generated from the ongoing operations, Mako is now in a much better position to deploy capital toward regional exploration. Indeed, San Albino is a relatively small asset, but producing ~40,000 ounces of gold per year at all-in sustaining costs below $1,400 per ounce provides the perfect platform for future growth. Mako's strategy is in sharp contrast to some of the junior explorers I have researched over the last few weeks. Without going into too much detail, I can mention Amex Exploration (AMX:CA) doing more than 600,000 meters of drilling without generating any form of revenue yet. In those cases, shareholder dilution unfortunately becomes the name of the game.

In a way, Mako Mining reminds me of Aya Gold & Silver (AYA:CA), a small silver producer I researched last month. As a reminder, Aya's flagship asset is the Zgounder pure-play silver mine located in Morocco. After reaching commercial production in December 2024, Aya now has a stable platform to support further growth. The operational team at Zgounder still has a lot of work to do on bringing operating costs lower, although the Feasibility Study was talking about an all-in sustaining cost of ~$10.00 per ounce of silver.

My 'Hold' rating reflects my optimism regarding Zgounder's free cash flow generation, but also my concerns about the reinvestment risks associated with the Boumadine Project.

With Zgounder's cash flows, Aya is reinvesting in regional exploration and advancing the Boumadine Project. In a completely different environment, Mako is employing the same strategy with its highly profitable San Albino Mine. As a top priority, capital is deployed into regional exploration to sustain production and cash flows, with the balance allocated to growth.

In my view, this is an attractive strategy as it avoids the serial dilution associated with pure-play exploration companies. As a result, Mako can grow on an organic basis without the need for constant capital injections. Of note, Mako purchased $4.7 million worth of shares as part of its buyback program in 2024. Purchases were not in substantial amounts, but it highlights the fact that Mako is generating excess liquidity instead of consuming it. There is no question that it creates a lot more room for error on the exploration front.

As indicated below, San Albino generated cash flows at the mine level of almost $55 million, after subtracting production costs of $38.2 million from total revenues of $91.6 million. With an average realized gold price of $2,361 throughout 2024, San Albino's cash-flow generation should increase quite meaningfully in the coming quarters. For a company with a ~$300 million market capitalization, free cash flows are about to become substantial, meaning that Mako's net cash balance of $5.0 million could grow much further.

Administrative Expenses & Executive Compensation

Another element that caught my attention on the annual income statement is the $8.6 million in general and administrative expenses, which is pretty good for a publicly-listed entity. To put this number in perspective, Aya Gold & Silver disclosed general and administrative expenses of $16.5 million over the same period. Indeed, Aya Gold & Silver is almost 5x larger based on market capitalization, but does it justify multi-million compensation packages for key management members?

As illustrated by the two compensation tables below, Mako's chief executive officer received a total compensation slightly above $1.0 million in 2024 compared to more than $6.6 million for Aya's CEO, Benoit Lasalle. For the key person running the show, we are talking about a compensation gap of 6.6x, and the difference becomes even larger as we go down the executive ranks. For example, Mako's CFO earned a total compensation of $440,000 in 2024 compared to more than $4.4 million for Aya's CFO.

How can we explain such a large difference in executive compensation? Does it mean that Mako is underpaying its management members, or is it the opposite, with Aya's management siphoning excessive value from shareholders? Everyone will have to answer this question for themselves, but in Mako's case, there is probably some discipline imposed by Wexford Capital, the company's largest shareholder with a 48% ownership interest. Additionally, Mako's CEO, Akiba Leisman, serves as a consultant for Wexford, indicating a close relationship between these two entities. In Aya's case, there is no controlling interest that could force some discipline on management compensation and overall administrative expenses. Of course, it is not the function of passive mutual funds and ETFs to enforce discipline.

To use an expression employed by a reader, the best way to make money with Aya is probably to become the CEO or a key executive. Mako seems to be much leaner, which is something I appreciate from the viewpoint of a prospective investor. At the end of the day, the only way I can make money as a minority shareholder is through a higher share price or dividend payments. Unfortunately, I am unlikely ever to receive millions worth of cheap stock options, so I must deploy my capital carefully and opportunistically.

After three years living alone in the woods, I now understand the energizing aspect of maintaining a lean cost structure and lifestyle. My lifestyle was a lot more bloated during my time in Toronto, and my mind was not as sharp. For a CEO with a multi-million-dollar package, where is the motivation to do anything outside of the box? After all, the share price would not matter very much at all.

Moss Mine Acquisition

In March 2024, Mako announced the completion of the Moss Acquisition, a small open-pit mine located in Arizona. Mako paid an aggregate purchase price of $6.5 million. After including interim production and the release of environmental bonds, Mako's net cash acquisition cost amounted to approximately $2.0 million, which represents just a few weeks' worth of cash flows from the San Albino Mine. Moss is already generating revenues, and Mako's management expects to improve asset performance over the coming quarters.

The acquisition of the producing Moss gold mine in Arizona, after the elimination of over US$ 60 million of existing liabilities associated with the mine, for a base purchase price of US$ 4.9 million, further reduced by US$ 1.5 million through the release of certain collateral, funded out of a fraction of this quarter’s corporate cash generation, is a testament to the powerful platform Mako has at its disposal. December 2024 Press Release

Given the price paid, the infrastructure already in place, the existing production, and the exploration potential, what is the downside for Mako's shareholders? Although Mako does not explicitly mention it, the offset likely includes environmental liabilities and reclamation costs. If it is indeed the case, compelling returns on invested capital are likely to be generated only if production can be maintained as far out into the future as possible. Expanding mine life is the best way to reduce the present value of those reclamation and rehabilitation costs.

In my view, paying a net cash consideration of only $2.0 million for Moss out of a bankruptcy proceeding is an indication of smart capital allocation. The risk/reward proposition of this transaction seems to be highly favourable to Mako's shareholders. After all, Moss demonstrated an ability to sustain production levels at ~30,000 ounces of gold per year since commercial production was declared in September 2018. As a reminder, San Albino produced ~40,000 ounces of gold in 2024.

According to the most recent Technical Report with an effective date of July 2021, the Moss Mine was estimated to contain approximately 490,000 ounces of gold at an average grade of 0.39 g/t. Although the head grade may look low at first sight, it is not atypical for heap leach operations in the region. It is also interesting to note that while Moss produces ~25% fewer ounces than San Albino, it is almost twice as big based on measured and indicated ounces. The future will tell, but I think Moss is a smart move coming from Mako's management team.

Eagle Mountain Project

In March 2024, Mako announced the acquisition of Goldsource Mines, owner of the Eagle Mountain Project located approximately 200 kilometres away from Georgetown, the capital of Guyana. Based on Mako's current share price of $3.90, the 13.2 million shares issued as part of the transaction are now worth slightly over $50 million. On a pro forma basis, Goldsource's shareholders owned approximately 17% of the combined entity.

In the oil and gas industry, the exploration success of Exxon Mobil (XOM) offshore Guyana has become legendary, following the announcement of the Liza discovery in May 2015. Since then, Exxon has announced major discoveries after major discoveries, and the company now expects to produce in excess of 1.3 million barrels of oil per day from Guyana by the end of 2027.

While the oil and gas industry is doing exceedingly well in the country, pretty much the same thing can be said about the local mining industry. As key players in the country, we find G Mining Ventures (GMIN:CA) with the Oko West Project, G2 Goldfields (GTWO:CA) with the OMZ Deposit, Omai Gold Mines (OMG:CA) with the Wenot Deposit and also Aris Mining (ARIS:CA) with its Toroparu Project. Over time, I plan to research all of those companies, starting probably with Aris Mining, given its close proximity to Collective Mining (CNL), a company I covered a few days ago.

If we return to Eagle Mountain, a Preliminary Economic Assessment on the project was published in March 2024. The report highlighted a resource base of approximately 1.2 million ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 1.2 g/t. The project also demonstrated its potential to deliver an attractive economic return, with an estimated internal rate of return of ~57% using a gold price of $1,850 per ounce. Similar to San Albino in Nicaragua and Moss in Arizona, Eagle Mountain would be mined through shallow open pit operations with an anticipated mine life of 15 years.

It is still early days for Eagle Mountain, but with initial capital costs estimated at $142 million and significant free cash flows coming from San Albino, this is an asset that Mako could put into production organically, without the need to issue additional shares. In this case, Mako's portfolio could grow from one producing asset as of the end of 2024 to three producing assets potentially by the end of the decade.

Conclusion

The San Albino mine forms the core pillar of Mako's organically funded growth strategy. Operational efficiency should improve in the next few quarters at the newly acquired Moss Mine in Arizona. Once the optimization is over, Mako will find itself with two cash-flowing assets and a development project in Guyana.

To be honest, I am quite impressed by Mako's strategy. If everything goes according to plan, which is a big if in the mining industry, Mako's production could surpass 100,000 ounces of gold per year somewhere around 2028. Considering the production volumes slightly below 40,000 ounces of gold in 2024, it would represent substantial growth rates.

My 'Buy' rating does not mean that I will rush to buy the stock on Monday morning. In fact, it simply means that at a certain price, this is an asset that I would like to own over the medium to long term. Along with gold prices, Mako's share price has rallied quite significantly since the start of 2025, and before initiating a new position, I would prefer to wait for some form of technical pullback first. Using current gold prices of almost $3,500, Mako looks cheap based on pretty much any valuation multiple. For example, in terms of free cash flow, we could easily be talking about a yield north of 20% in 2025, and potentially much higher if gold can maintain some of its recent gains. However, one element to keep in mind when thinking about Mako's valuation multiples is the relatively low reserve-life index, which is generally associated with lower multiples.

To deepen my understanding of the gold mining industry in Nicaragua, I will now turn my focus to Calibre Mining (CXB:CA), one of the largest producers in the country. As mentioned in other articles, if you would enjoy coverage on a specific miner, please leave a comment below, as I may fast-track the most popular requests.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UWM Holdings: Collect A 9.6% Dividend Yield While Waiting For Housing Market Recovery
data.description:
I have been following UWM Holdings (NYSE:UWMC) for quite a few years now, as its story fascinates me. The company, which operates as United Wholesale Mortgage, came public via a massive SPAC deal back in 2021. That deal, like many SPACs, suffered from unfortunate timing and shares traded down almost immediately once the transaction closed.

Years later, UWMC has struggled to regain the initial $10 transaction price, with the stock heading back under $5 recently:
